title: Free IBU Beer Calculator Tinseth 2026 | Hacé Cuentas
h1: Beer IBU Calculator Using Tinseth Formula
description: Calculate beer IBU (bitterness) units with the Tinseth formula. Input hops, alpha acids, boil time & volume. Free homebrewing calculator.
intro: **IBUs (International Bitterness Units)** measure beer bitterness in mg/L of iso-alpha acids. The **Tinseth formula (1997)** is the homebrewing standard because it adjusts hop utilization based on wort density and boil time. This calculator lets you input hops, alpha acid percentage, boil time, and volume to get total IBUs. Perfect for cloning commercial styles or designing balanced recipes.
keyTakeaway: **Tinseth: IBU = Utilization × (AA% × grams × 1000) / (Volume × 100)**. American IPAs: 40–70 IBU, Lagers: 20–30, Imperial Stouts: 50–90.
useCases: You're designing an IPA recipe and need to dial in the bitterness target.
You switched hop varieties and need to adjust grams to match the original IBU.
You want to clone a commercial beer and match its IBU profile.
You're testing different boil times and want to see the bitterness impact.
You're making multiple hop additions and need the total IBU across all of them.

fields.label: Hop grams
fields.help: Typical bittering additions: 15–50g. Total per boil may reach 300g.

fields.label: Alpha acids (%AA)
fields.help: Alpha acid percentage from your hop supplier. Typically 5–15%.

fields.label: OG (original gravity)
fields.help: Wort density affects hop utilization. Higher gravity reduces bitterness extraction.

example.title: Example: American Pale Ale
example.result: With 30g Cascade at 60 minutes, you get ~15 IBU. An American Pale Ale targets 30–45 IBU.

explanation:
## What Are IBUs?

**IBUs** measure milligrams of **iso-alpha acids** per liter. During the boil, hop alpha acids isomerize and contribute bitterness to the finished beer.

## Tinseth Formula

```
IBU = Utilization × (AA% × grams × 1000) / (Volume_L × 100)
Utilization = (1.65 × 0.000125^(OG−1)) × (1 − e^(−0.04×time)) / 4.15
```

Higher wort density reduces utilization, so a dense IPA needs more hops to hit the same IBU.

## IBU by Beer Style (BJCP)

| Style | Typical IBU |
|---|---|
| American Light Lager | 8–12 |
| Hefeweizen | 10–15 |
| Pale Ale | 30–50 |
| IPA | 40–70 |
| Double IPA | 60–100 |
| Stout | 25–50 |
| Imperial Stout | 50–90 |
| Pilsner | 25–45 |

## Utilization by Boil Time

| Minutes | Utilization (OG 1.050) |
|---|---|
| 5 | 5% |
| 15 | 12% |
| 30 | 18% |
| 60 | 24% |
| 90 | 27% |

## Types of Hop Additions

- **Bittering**: 60–90 min boil, high IBU, minimal aroma.
- **Flavor**: 15–30 min, balanced bittering and flavor.
- **Aroma**: 0–10 min or whirlpool, little to no IBU, maximum aroma.
- **Dry hop**: fermentation vessel, 0 IBU, maximum aroma.

## BU:GU Ratio

Bitterness Units to Gravity Units ratio: <0.5 (sweet), 0.5–0.8 (balanced), 0.8–1.2 (bitter), >1.2 (very bitter).

See [hop utilization by boil time](/hop-utilization-boil-time).

faq.q: Why use the Tinseth formula over other IBU calculations?
faq.a: Tinseth (1997) is more accurate for higher-gravity beers (OG >1.050) compared to formulas like Rager, which tend to overestimate. It's now the industry standard in modern brewing software.

faq.q: Do IBUs decrease as beer ages?
faq.a: Yes. IBU levels typically drop 5–15% due to oxidation. A 60 IBU beer might drop to 50–55 IBU within 3–6 months of storage.
Very little—less than 5 IBU even with high rates (6g/L). Alpha acids need heat to isomerize into iso-alpha acids and create bitterness.

faq.q: What is cohumulone and why does it matter?
faq.a: Cohumulone is the fraction of alpha acids that produces harsh, astringent bitterness. Hops with <25% cohumulone (like Saaz or Tettnanger) deliver smooth, refined bitterness.

faq.q: Do hop pellets give more IBU than whole hops?
faq.a: Yes. Pellets are milled and compressed, giving roughly 10% higher utilization than whole hops. Some calculators apply a 1.1× adjustment factor.

faq.q: At what temperature do alpha acids isomerize?
faq.a: Alpha acids start isomerizing above 80°C (176°F). At a rolling boil (100°C / 212°F), isomerization is optimal. Whirlpooling at 80°C for 30+ minutes can add unexpected IBUs.

faq.q: How do I adjust a recipe if my IBU is too high or too low?
faq.a: Reduce hop grams, choose lower alpha acid varieties, or shorten boil time to decrease IBU. Increase any of these to add more bitterness. Remember that wort gravity also affects utilization—a bigger beer needs more hops for the same IBU.

faq.q: What's a good BU:GU ratio for balanced beer?
faq.a: BU:GU is bitterness units divided by gravity units. Most balanced beers sit between 0.5 and 0.8. Below 0.5 tastes malt-forward; above 1.2 tastes very bitter.
